text: Hello, I'm sharing an interesting case from a few months ago of a young lady with Crohn's disease who's developed an impassable stricture in the sigmoid colon. Isolated area of disease of the sigmoid colon biopsy is consistent with Crohn's. No other colonic or small bowel disease. Interestingly and completely unrelated, she has full thickness rectal prolapse and I elected to repair that simultaneously. Although for patients with prolapse alone, I no longer do resection rectopexies in favor of rectopexy alone.
In this case, I'm doing a resection of the sigmoid for her Crohn's and a rectopexy for the prolapse.

The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection, carefully identifying the left ureter before isolating the superior hemorrhoidal artery or inferior mesenteric artery (IMA). The vessel is thinned out and clipped before division, using an energy device for sealing. The dissection continues laterally while double-checking the ureter's position.

The surgeon attempts to elevate the rectus sigmoid and rectum from the pelvis by releasing adhesions on the left pelvic sidewall. The dissection joins the previously medially dissected plane, progressing towards the area where the bowel is adherent to the left pelvic sidewall and the left adnexa.

The surgeon performs a full posterior dissection in the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane, having released the left side. The dissection involves posterior mobilization and right lateral and anterior mobilization, while leaving the left lateral side intact.

The surgeon uses a ligature device to carefully dissect the adherent tissue from the left adnexa, employing a finger fracture technique to separate it from the pelvic sidewall. The pericolic fat and mesorectum are left on the sidewall to avoid the ureter, which is not catheterized in this case. The surgeon identifies the distal margin location and elevates the tissue out of the pelvis, preparing to complete the right lateral and anterior dissection. A deep cul-de-sac is visible in the surgical field.

A suture is placed to suspend the uterus, facilitating the dissection. The procedure is performed using a three-port technique with an umbilical Hassan for the ten-millimeter camera, a right lower quadrant twelve-millimeter trocar, and a right mid-quadrant five-millimeter trocar.

The surgeon re-enters the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane to facilitate the anterior dissection in the rectovaginal septum. The dissection progresses as low as possible, with digital palpation confirming the depth. The surgeon then completes the posterior dissection.

The surgeon follows the curve of the sacrum to the coccyx, clearing the recto-sigmoid mesentery by putting it under tension. The bowel is exteriorized through a small Pfannenstiel incision, and an anvil is placed for a stapled anastomosis.

The surgeon performs a hand-sewn anastomosis, preferring robotic assistance for ease. The proximal mesentery is taken, and blood supply is checked before dividing distally. The bowel is exteriorized, and the anvil is placed. The surgeon uses ICG to check perfusion and places a clip at the area of demarcation for visibility under white light. The distal section is then stapled.

The surgeon performs a rectopexy using 2-0 silk sutures on an SH needle, taking a bite of the periosteum and rectal mesentery without including the bowel wall. An assistant retracts the bowel over the promontory to facilitate suturing, which becomes easier after the first stitch is placed.

The surgeon places a second suture, exposing the periosteum on the bone to avoid thickened vascular tissue. Bipolar energy is used to cauterize visible blood vessels before suturing. The second stitch is placed lower on the rectum to aid in its elevation, ensuring secure attachment despite the stitch snapping.

I didn't use to close this peritoneal opening. I used to leave it and I've actually taken patients back to the operating room, not my own, but Patients have had this operation elsewhere where this wasn't closed and I've had small bowel stuck to raw surfaces down there leading to a bowel obstruction. As well, some of my original postings in this group from several years ago where I didn't close it, I had a lot of commentary from many observers of these cases. that it's a good idea to close it. So ever since then I have been closing it. I've learned from you guys as well. Interestingly, I have a colleague who did one of these cases a few months ago and they told me that the patient developed a bowel obstruction due to the the nubbin of the tail of the V-lock sticking up, which is certainly something that I have seen in a lot of the hernia society posts, and it is one of the concerns of using V-lock. That patient of my colleagues needed to go back to the operating room and ended up with an ileoplectomy due to the way things got trapped due to the adhesions from that little stitch. I used to close these actually just with a vical suture, and obviously it works just fine, and maybe you could eliminate the concern for the adherence to the end of that tail. I try to bury it in the fat and then cover it over with some momentum, and fortunately I haven't experienced any of those issues yet, but obviously it's a concern. I don't know if there's really any right way to do it. This case went very nicely. I was very happy the patient was able to be discharged on post-operative day one.
The surgeon performs a leak test on the anastomosis, observing for any signs of leakage, which are absent, indicating a successful seal.
I don't leave drains for any of my anterior resections anymore. I'm very selective in that. And I hope you enjoyed.
